title: STANDARD FOR WARE POTATOES CXS 339-2020 Adopted in 2020.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR WARE POTATOES CXS 339-2020 Adopted in 2020.
sections.summary: Document title only: Standard for Ware Potatoes (CXS 339‑2020), adopted 2020; not a substantive provision.

Defines the standard's purpose to set quality requirements for ware potatoes after preparation and packaging, allows slight lack of freshness/turgidity and slight deterioration post‑packaging, and places responsibility on the holder/seller to ensure products are marketed only in conformity with the standard.
Applies the Standard to commercial varieties of Solanum tuberosum L. supplied fresh after preparation and packaging and explicitly excludes ware potatoes for industrial processing and early potatoes.
Sets provisions concerning quality, including minimum requirements, minimum maturity, optional classification, and tolerances for ware potatoes.

Lists detailed minimum requirements: tubers must be intact, sound, fresh/firm, clean and practically free of visible foreign matter and pests, practically free from pest damage, free from abnormal external moisture and foreign smell, free from temperature damage, with sprouts ≤3 mm, and without specified external or internal defects (with numerical depth/area limits for certain defects); they must withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition.
Requires ware potatoes to be sufficiently developed with cured skin, taking account of variety/commercial type and growing area (minimum maturity requirement).
States that classification is optional and, when applied pursuant to Section 5 tolerances, ware potatoes may be classified into 'Extra' Class, Class I or Class II.

Defines sizing options for ware potatoes (by diameter, count, weight or trading practices) with labeling required if using trading practices; provides an optional equatorial diameter method and size-code ranges (A: >80 mm; B: 35–80 mm; C: 25–75 mm; D: 18–24 mm) and limits size non‑uniformity in sales packages up to 5 kg to a maximum 30 mm difference between smallest and largest tuber.

Specifies allowable percentages of defective ware potatoes by class: total tolerances (Extra Class 5%, Class I 10%, Class II 10%); specific limits for frozen/decay/soft rot/internal breakdown (Extra 1%, Class I 1%, Class II 2%), extraneous matter (0.25%/0.5%/0.5%), and soil (0.25% across classes), and lists defect types (cuts, bruises, sub‑epidermal stains, scab, sprouts, green coloration) subject to these tolerances; additional tolerances permit 2% for other varieties and 10% for off‑size, with the sum of specified defect categories not to exceed the total tolerance and Class II tolerances applying when not classified.
Heading only — introductory title for presentation provisions; no substantive requirements provided in this section.

Requires each package or bulk lot of ware potatoes to be uniform in origin, variety/commercial type, quality, size (if sized) and optionally cooking type, with the visible portion representative of the whole; allows packing distinct varieties of different skin colours together (except green) only if uniform in quality and origin for each variety.

Requires ware potatoes to be packed so as to protect them, using food‑grade, clean materials that will not cause external or internal damage, permits paper or stamps if printed with non‑toxic ink/glue, and mandates compliance with the Code of Practice for Packaging and Transport (CXC 44‑1995).
Specifies that containers must provide appropriate quality, hygiene, ventilation and strength for handling, shipping and preservation of ware potatoes and must be free of foreign matter and odour.
Heading only — introductory title for marking and labelling provisions; no substantive requirements provided in this section.

States that consumer package labelling must meet the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) and that additional specific provisions apply (details provided in subsequent clauses).
If the ware potatoes are not visible from outside the package, the package must be labelled with the name ‘Ware Potato’ and should indicate the variety and/or commercial type.

Requires declaration of country of origin (optionally district or place name) and, for mixtures of distinctly different varieties from different origins, the country of origin for each variety must be indicated next to that variety’s name.

Non‑retail packages must bear legible, indelible particulars grouped on the same side and visible from outside or on a label; for bulk transport these particulars must appear on an accompanying document attached visibly to the transport vehicle or as an accessible machine‑readable electronic solution.
Requires name and address of the exporter, packer and/or dispatcher on non‑retail packages, with an identification code optional.
Non‑retail packages must be labelled with the name of the produce and may include variety and/or commercial type; tuber shape (e.g., oval, round, long) may be optionally indicated.

Requires country of origin (and optionally district/place name) on non‑retail packages and, for mixtures of distinct varieties from different origins, the country of origin for each variety must be shown next to that variety.
Commercial identification on packages may include class (if classified), size (if sized), and optionally cooking type, flesh colour and tuber shape.
States that an official inspection mark is optional; no further details provided.

Requires ware potatoes to comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and with maximum levels for contaminants and toxins as set out in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995).

sections.section: 8.1 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum residue limits for pesticides established by the Codex Alimentarius Commission.
sections.summary: States that the produce must comply with Codex‑established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

sections.section: 8.2 The produce covered by this Standard shall comply with the maximum levels of the contaminants and toxins in accordance with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).
sections.summary: States that the produce must comply with maximum levels for contaminants and toxins in accordance with the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995).

Recommends that handling and preparation follow the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1‑1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53‑2003) and other relevant Codex texts, and that produce comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21‑1997.

sections.section: 9.1 It is recommended that the produce covered by the provisions of this Standard be prepared and handled in accordance with the appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003), and other relevant Codex texts such as Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.
sections.summary: Recommends that the produce covered by this Standard be prepared and handled in accordance with appropriate sections of the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables (CXC 53-2003) and other relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice and Codes of Practice.

sections.section: 9.2 The produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
sections.summary: States that the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
